The blue-team questions that govern every service-account deployment have the same answers here: the OS account, the ACLs, and the audit log tell the story, independent of any AI-layer configuration.

BYOH and agent teams operate inside this same boundary. The harness (whichever one the brain runs — Claude Code, Codex, Ollama) is a guest inside the brain OS account and is bounded by those ACLs regardless of which harness it is. Agent teams coordinate multiple specialized agents, each scoped to their own permission boundaries. Model preferences route different task tiers to appropriate model groups. All of it operates within the OS-enforced boundary — swapping harnesses, teams, or models does not change what the brain can reach. See §1.3 for BYOH details, §6 for harness configuration, and §7 for model preferences and agent teams.

docs.title: 1.2 Brains in the Security Model
docs.text:
A brain is a separate OS user account scoped to its own directory subtree. This is not a metaphor — the brain user is a real local account with a generated 64-character password stored in the OS keystore, never printed anywhere.

The security model for a brain at provisioning time:

What Enforcement Full access to its own workspace (brains/<name>/) brains_group ACL grants full control; no other account has access Read + execute on $HORIZON_BIN and skills_bin brains group grant — the "can use Horizon.AIOS tooling" gate Explicit Deny on sbin, skills_sbin, and logs Non-inherited Deny ACE — cannot be overridden by any Allow Cannot write to the Horizon.AIOS system layer Deny ACE on $HORIZON_SYSTEM; OS-enforced regardless of what the harness says

The critical property: even a fully compromised harness cannot escalate past the OS boundary. The Deny ACEs are not app-layer promises. They are OS-level restrictions that the harness runs inside — not controls the harness can modify.

A brain's access to data and tooling is zero by default. Every grant must be explicit and is answerable from the provisioning record (.aios_provision.json in the brain's folder).

Why this matters — the problem being solved. When agentic workflows run under a human user account, they inherit everything that account can access: the full filesystem, credentials, network resources, other applications. A misbehaving or compromised agent is a compromised user session — indistinguishable from the user, with the user's full privileges, and no containment boundary. This is the security gap that dedicated brain OS accounts close. The brain account has only what it was explicitly given. Nothing more can be reached regardless of what the harness instructs or what prompt injection attempts.

For isolation verification commands, see $HORIZON_DOCS/security/brain_isolation_test.md. To re-apply the ACL model after structural changes, run /harden.

docs.title: 1.3 Bring Your Own Harness in the Security Model
docs.text:
BYOH means the harness — Claude Code, Codex, Ollama, or any tool that reads agents.md — is a guest inside the Horizon.AIOS layer, not the layer itself. Harnesses do not need to be "plugged in" to Horizon.AIOS; they inherit context automatically by reading the directory structure they are pointed at.

How a harness gets its context depends on which harness it is:

  • Popular vendor harnesses (Claude Code, Codex, Ollama, LM Studio, etc.) — Horizon.AIOS onboarding actively redirects the harness's default configuration directory to point at the Horizon.AIOS root. After onboarding, every launch of that harness picks up the full Horizon.AIOS context chain without further wiring. Vendor-specific wiring lives in $HORIZON_SYSTEM/harness_configs/<vendor>/.
  • Windowed / sandboxed harnesses (Claude Desktop, Codex desktop app, etc.) — These harnesses run in a sandbox and cannot have their configuration directory repointed to the Horizon.AIOS root. Instead, point the harness at the specific project or brain folder you are working in. Because Horizon.AIOS CLAUDE.md files cascade down the directory tree, the harness will inherit Horizon.AIOS context from the folder hierarchy automatically — you are working within the harness's constraints rather than redirecting it. As of initial development (June 2026), this is the required pattern for windowed harnesses.
  • Any other harness — No active redirection is needed. Any harness that reads agents.md and faithfully imports the files it references will receive the full Horizon.AIOS context chain. The agents.md at $HORIZON_ROOT/agents.md is the cross-harness entry point; a compliant harness needs only to load it.

The vendor-neutrality goal. Horizon.AIOS is designed so that CLAUDE.md imports agents.md, and agents.md is where the substantive Horizon.AIOS instructions live — making the system as harness-neutral as possible. Any harness that can load agents.md and honor its @-imports gets equivalent context to Claude Code.

Known limitation (as of June 2026). The Claude Code harness does not honor @-imports declared inside agents.md — only @-imports inside CLAUDE.md files are resolved by the harness. As a result, files that agents.md intends to import must currently be duplicated as @-imports in CLAUDE.md to be loaded. This is a harness limitation, not a Horizon.AIOS design decision, and is tracked as a known gap pending upstream resolution.

A brain's harness runs as the brain OS user and is therefore bounded by the brain's OS permissions. Swapping harnesses does not change what the brain can reach — the ACLs are on the filesystem, not on any harness-specific config.

The implication: the security model is the same regardless of which harness the brain runs. A brain running Claude Code and a brain running Codex have the same filesystem restrictions. The harness governs what the AI can do within those bounds; the OS governs what the account can touch.

See $HORIZON_DOCS/philosophy.md §7 (BYOH) and $HORIZON_DOCS/security_architecture_invariants.md §5 (the harness-is-bounded-by-the-OS invariant).

docs.title: 1.4 Deployment Baseline and Multiple Horizon.AIOS Instances
docs.text:
Secure baseline. The most secure deployment is one where an administrator installs Horizon.AIOS and every user on the machine — human operators and AI brains alike — onboards to it. In this model, all users inherit the same security posture: their harness configurations point at the same Horizon.AIOS root, and their access is governed by OS-level ACLs, not by who happens to own the process. Flesh-and-blood users operate under the same structural constraints as agentic brain accounts. The boundary between "human user" and "AI agent" is the account; the security model treats them identically.

Multiple Horizon.AIOS instances. It is possible to run more than one Horizon.AIOS installation on a single machine. There is currently no tooling for this. The user must manually define unique OS environment variables for each instance (e.g., $HORIZON_ROOT_002, $HORIZON_SYSTEM_002) and update all references across every instance by hand. The instance whose path is bound to $HORIZON_ROOT is the one that harness configurations (Claude Code, Ollama, etc.) will point at — whichever instance owns $HORIZON_ROOT is the active instance. Multi-instance management tooling is a known gap.

docs.title: 2. Building and Configuring a Brain
docs.text: A note on the harness. A harness is the AI tool that runs inside a brain's OS account (Claude Code, Codex, Ollama, etc.). Horizon.AIOS does not prescribe which harness you use — it provides the structure that any harness can run on. When you provision a brain, the harness is automatically wired to the brain's context (its ~/.claude/ points at the brain workspace). You do not need to manually connect a harness to Horizon.AIOS; the wiring happens at provisioning time. See Section 6 for the full BYOH treatment.

docs.title: 2.1 Provisioning
docs.text:
Administrative rights are required to provision a brain. The provisioning script creates OS user accounts and sets filesystem ACLs — operations that require administrator or root privileges. On Windows, run the command from an elevated PowerShell prompt (right-click → "Run as Administrator") or as a user with local administrator rights. On Linux/macOS, prefix with sudo or run as root.

Brain provisioning is a single elevated command:

# Windows (Administrator PowerShell)
python "$env:HORIZON_SYSTEM\sbin\horizon_aios_create_brain.py" <brain-name>

# Linux / macOS
sudo python "$HORIZON_SYSTEM/sbin/horizon_aios_create_brain.py" <brain-name>

Or use the /create-brain skill from an owner session. The script creates the OS user account, the brains group (if absent), a per-brain group, the workspace folder with correct ACLs, and a shell profile that loads the HORIZON_* environment variables on login. A .aios_provision.json manifest is written into the brain's folder.

After provisioning, the brain user's ~/.claude/ is already pointed at brains/<name>/.claude/ — the harness is wired to Horizon.AIOS at provisioning time, not separately. There is no additional "connect harness to Horizon.AIOS" step.

Brain names must match ^[a-z][a-z0-9_]{1,31}$. Use descriptive names that reflect the brain's function: code_reviewer, inbox_triage, research_assistant.

docs.title: 2.2 The Brain's Home Directory
docs.text:
The brain's workspace lives at $HORIZON_ROOT/brains/<name>/. This is the brain's entire world by default. Inside it:

brains/<name>/
  .claude/
    CLAUDE.md          # thin entry; @-imports brain_core.md and local overrides
    settings.json      # harness-layer permissions (defense-in-depth only; real isolation is the ACLs)
    skills -> $HORIZON_SYSTEM/skills_bin/   # symlink; brain sees group-readable skills only
  brain_core.md        # brain identity, persona, scope — edit this after provisioning
  .aios_provision.json  # provisioning manifest; do not delete
  memory/               # the brain's memory system — your design, your choice
  projects -> ...        # optional: symlinks to project folders
  skills/               # optional: brain-only skills not shared with other brains
  tools/                 # optional: scripts, MCP configs, credentials the brain needs

docs.title: 2.3 Building the Memory System
docs.text:
Horizon.AIOS does not implement a memory system. The brains/<name>/ directory is a secure, isolated container. What you put inside it is your decision entirely.

Common patterns:

  • File-based memory — Markdown documents, calibration files, few-shot examples, domain knowledge. The simplest form. The brain's brain_core.md tells it where to look and what the files mean.
  • Vector index — a ChromaDB, FAISS, or similar index stored in a subdirectory. The brain has a retrieval script in tools/ that wraps it.
  • Structured knowledge base — SQLite database, JSON knowledge store, or any format the brain's tooling can query.
  • Conversation history — the harness writes transcripts to brains/<name>/.claude/projects/ automatically (via the ~/.claude/projects redirect). These are available to the brain across sessions.

The simplest pattern to start with: have your harness maintain a set of indexed Markdown files inside brains/<name>/. Point brain_core.md at an index.md that lists the files and their purpose. At the end of each session the brain updates whichever files are relevant — no external tooling required. The file-based pattern is what the example_Horizon.AIOS_Docs_Brain ships with; inspect brains/example_Horizon.AIOS_Docs_Brain/ for a working reference.

The memory system is what makes the brain an expert rather than a stateless query processor. When the brain's expertise is consolidated in self-contained artifacts within its directory — not embedded in a specific harness or model fine-tuning — the brain is portable: swap the harness, swap the model, move to another machine, and the expertise travels with the directory.

See $HORIZON_DOCS/philosophy.md §6 for the design rationale and $HORIZON_DOCS/system/memory.md for how harness transcripts are redirected.

docs.title: 2.4 Giving the Brain Access to Tooling
docs.text:
Bring the tooling to the brain — don't expect the brain to range for it.

Tools are delivered into the brain's boundary, externally provisioned. Every tool the brain holds is auditable and revocable. The provisioning question: "What tooling does this brain have?" is answerable from what was brought to it.

Practical provisioning:

1.1 Brain-only skills — place in brains/<name>/skills/. Skills here are available only to this brain, not shared with other brains or the owner. Use this for highly specialized or sensitive skills scoped to this brain's purpose.

1.2 Scripts and CLI tools — place in brains/<name>/tools/. These are files the brain's OS user owns and can execute. Keep them deterministic. The brain coordinates the tool; the tool does the computation.

1.3 MCP servers — MCP configuration lives in the brain's settings.json under the mcpServers key. Since the brain's ~/.claude/settings.json points at brains/<name>/.claude/settings.json, MCP servers configured there are available only to that brain.

{
  "mcpServers": {
    "filesystem": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem", "/path/to/allowed/dir"]
    }
  }
}

Scope MCP server paths to what the brain actually needs. An MCP server that exposes the full filesystem defeats the isolation model.

1.4 Credentials — store in the OS-native credential store via horizon_aios_brain_credential.py, or place credential files inside brains/<name>/. Never store credentials in committed files or environment variables that could leak.

1.5 Network access — the brain OS user has the same network access as any local account by default. To restrict it, apply OS-level firewall rules or network policies per account. Horizon.AIOS does not manage network access; that is a standard IT infrastructure concern.

1.6 Filesystem access beyond the brain's home — grant access to additional paths explicitly. On Windows: icacls <path> /grant <brain-name>:(OI)(CI)RX. On Unix: add the brain user to a group that owns the target directory, or set ACLs with setfacl. Document every grant in the brain's provisioning record.

docs.title: 2.5 Running the Harness as the Brain User
docs.text:
The intent of brain accounts. Brain OS user accounts are the designated accounts for running automated processes and long-running agentic workflows. This is the core of the security model separation: interactive humans run under their own accounts; automations and agents run under brain accounts. In the ideal deployment, an administrator installs Horizon.AIOS and all users — including human operators — onboard to it and operate within the same ACL-enforced posture. Human accounts get the same structural treatment as brain accounts: access is explicit, auditable, and revocable. The distinction between a human user and a brain is the account's intended use, not a difference in the security model applied to it.

The harness must run as the brain OS user — not as the owner — to enforce the isolation boundary. The harness reads configuration from ~/.claude/, and the brain's ~/.claude/ is symlinked to its workspace, so the harness automatically gets the brain's context when launched as the brain.

Interactive use:

# Windows — launch Claude Code as the brain
runas /user:<brain-name> claude

# Linux / macOS — switch to the brain user and launch
su - <brain-name>
claude

On Windows, the brain's password can be retrieved (as Administrator) for runas:

python "$env:HORIZON_SYSTEM\sbin\horizon_aios_brain_credential.py" get <brain-name> --show

Automated / unattended use:

For scheduled tasks or daemons, provision the brain with a logon right:

python $HORIZON_SYSTEM/sbin/horizon_aios_create_brain.py <brain-name> --automation scheduled

Then register a Windows Task Scheduler task or Linux cron job whose user account is the brain. The task launches the harness directly (claude -p "<prompt>"). Because the task runs as the brain user, all isolation guarantees apply — the scheduled harness cannot access anything the brain's ACLs deny it.

See $HORIZON_DOCS/deployment/brain_automation.md for the full three-tier logon rights model (none / scheduled / daemon) and $HORIZON_DOCS/deployment/desktop.md for the complete wiring walkthrough.

docs.title: 3.1 What Projects Are
docs.text:
$HORIZON_PROJECTS ($HORIZON_ROOT/Projects/) is the designated workspace for non-agentic work — the things you work on, not the machinery that does the working. Think of it as the Horizon.AIOS equivalent of "My Documents" or ~/: your personal workspace, independent of the system layer.

What belongs in $HORIZON_PROJECTS: - Code repositories — the software you are writing or maintaining - Working copies — checked-out sources, forks, vendor code - Output locations — where agentic workflows deposit their results when they need a persistent home - Research, notes, and documents — anything that is content rather than tooling

What does not belong in $HORIZON_PROJECTS: skills, brain configuration, Horizon.AIOS tooling, or anything that defines how work gets done. That belongs in brains or the Horizon.AIOS system layer.

docs.title: 3.2 The Brains / Projects Distinction
docs.text:
The mental model is straightforward:

Analogy Contains Agentic? brains/ C:\Program Files\ / /opt/ — installed applications Configuration, skills, tooling, workflows Yes — this is where agents run Projects/ My Documents / ~/ (/home/<user>/) — user workspace Code repos, working copies, output No — this is what gets worked on

On Linux the analogy is direct: /opt/ holds installed software — system-managed, static, belongs to the OS layer. ~/ (your home directory, /home/<user>/) holds your personal workspace. brains/ maps to /opt/; Projects/ maps to ~/. Neither pollutes the other; each has its own ownership, its own git history, and its own change rate.

Brains and projects are not intrinsically linked. A brain does not need a project to operate — it may work entirely within its own workspace. A project does not need a brain — you can open a project in your own session and work in it directly. The connection is explicit and optional: when an agentic workflow needs to read or write a project, you grant access and wire the symlink.

This separation is what keeps the security model clean. The Horizon.AIOS layer (config, skills, hooks) lives in the OS repo. Code lives in project repos. Brain machinery lives in brain workspaces. Each category has its own git history, its own ACLs, and its own change rate — none of them pollute each other.

docs.title: 3.3 Practical Patterns for Project Folders
docs.text:
Each project folder inside $HORIZON_PROJECTS is an independent directory. Give it its own git history with git init — from that point the OS repo ignores it entirely:

mkdir "$env:HORIZON_PROJECTS\my-api"
cd "$env:HORIZON_PROJECTS\my-api"
git init

No .gitignore entry is needed. The OS repo pre-commit hook automatically drops any nested .git folder from tracking.

Common layouts:

$HORIZON_PROJECTS/
  my-api/           # git repo — backend service
  my-frontend/      # git repo — frontend
  shared-libs/      # git repo — shared code
  research-notes/   # plain folder — documents, no git needed
  agent-output/     # output target for an agentic workflow

NTFS directory symlinks. A project folder can be a symlink to a path elsewhere on the machine or a network share. From the Horizon.AIOS perspective it looks like a normal folder:

# Windows — directory symlink (run as administrator)
New-Item -ItemType SymbolicLink -Path "$env:HORIZON_PROJECTS\my-project" -Target "\\fileserver\share\my-project"

Cloud-synced storage. OneDrive, Google Drive, Dropbox, or cloud provider storage can be mounted or synced at a project folder path. Horizon.AIOS sees a local folder. For what to exclude from sync, see $HORIZON_DOCS/cloud_sync_exclusions.md.

docs.title: 3.4 Giving a Brain Access to a Project
docs.text:
Default: brain users have no access to $HORIZON_PROJECTS. No implicit grant exists. A brain cannot see, read, or write any project folder unless an administrator explicitly grants it. This is not a configuration choice — it is the baseline enforced by OS ACLs.

This default matters. The threat model Horizon.AIOS addresses is the one created when agentic workflows run under a human user account: those workflows inherit everything that account can touch — files, network resources, credentials, other applications — with no containment boundary. A compromised or misbehaving agent is a compromised user session. Brain OS accounts exist precisely to break this assumption: an agent runs with exactly the access it needs and nothing more.

When a brain legitimately needs project access, it is an explicit administrative act:

Important: Brains have no access to any Project folder by default — this is OS-enforced, not a setting. Every grant must be an explicit administrative act. The commands below are required any time a brain needs to read or write a project.

# Grant read+execute access (run as administrator)
icacls "$env:HORIZON_PROJECTS\my-api" /grant <brain-name>:(OI)(CI)RX

# Optionally symlink the project into the brain's workspace (run as administrator)
New-Item -ItemType SymbolicLink `
  -Path "$env:HORIZON_ROOT\brains\<brain-name>\my-api" `
  -Target "$env:HORIZON_PROJECTS\my-api"

The grant is auditable from the brain's provisioning record. The brain can read and write within the project folder, but the Horizon.AIOS system layer remains protected by its own ACLs regardless of what the brain does inside the project. The access is scoped to that folder — not to $HORIZON_PROJECTS as a whole.

docs.title: 3.5 Project-Level Horizon.AIOS Overrides
docs.text:
To customize Horizon.AIOS behavior for a specific project, drop an aios_overrides.md at the project root:

cp "$HORIZON_SYSTEM/templates/aios_overrides.md" "$env:HORIZON_PROJECTS/my-api/aios_overrides.md"

This can override handoffs_dir, objectives_dir, project_display_name, and other per-project settings. It does not affect the OS-layer security model or the brain isolation boundary.

Project-specific AI instructions go in $HORIZON_PROJECTS/my-api/.claude/CLAUDE.md. Claude Code loads these on top of the global CLAUDE.md when working inside that project.

docs.title: 4.1 The Problem They Solve
docs.text:
Every Claude Code session starts fresh. Context that mattered in the previous session is gone. For short tasks this is fine. For multi-session work — a complex feature, a weeks-long investigation, an evolving project — you need a way to carry state forward without manually re-explaining it every time.

Horizon.AIOS provides two complementary tools for this: handoffs (point-in-time session state) and objectives (durable, evolving goals).

docs.title: 4.2 /handoff
docs.text:
A handoff is a structured Markdown document that captures where a session ended and where the next one should pick up. Run /handoff at the end of any significant session.

The handoff document contains: - What was accomplished in this session - What decisions were made and why - What is deferred or blocked - The recommended entry point for the next session - A link to the active objective (if one exists)

Handoffs write to $HORIZON_ROOT/handoffs/ by default (configurable per project via aios_overrides.md). At the start of a new session on the same topic, read the most recent handoff to restore context.

Ephemeral and machine-local. The handoffs/ directory is gitignored — handoffs are never committed, synced, or backed up automatically. They exist only on this machine. If you uninstall or migrate Horizon.AIOS without backing up $HORIZON_ROOT/handoffs/, they are gone.

Usage:

/handoff

Claude will ask clarifying questions if needed, then write the document. No flags or arguments are required for a basic handoff. The skill is in skills_bin/ and available to all brains.

docs.title: 4.3 /objective
docs.text:
An objective is a durable goal document that persists across many sessions and many handoffs. Where a handoff captures a moment in time, an objective captures the sustained intent — what you are ultimately trying to accomplish and why.

Objectives live in $HORIZON_ROOT/objectives/ (configurable). They are plain Markdown files numbered sequentially. Handoffs reference objectives by number, so the goal survives even as the tactical details evolve across sessions.

Durable-but-ephemeral. Objectives are not permanent. They are pruned automatically by horizon_aios_maintain_logs.py based on the AIOS_OBJECTIVES_MAX_DAYS retention window. There is no "close" or "done" concept — an objective retires by going stale. To keep an objective alive, you must actively update it with /objective update N <note> — this refreshes the modification time and resets the retention clock. Only /objective update refreshes the modification time. /objective show does not — reading an objective does not reset the clock. Like handoffs, the objectives/ directory is gitignored and machine-local — never committed or auto-backed-up.

Warning: Reading an objective with /objective show does NOT reset its retention clock. If you are actively using an objective but only reading it, it will be silently pruned when the retention window expires. Run /objective update N <note> periodically to keep it alive.

Creating an objective:

/objective create "Migrate the authentication system to JWT by end of Q3"

Listing objectives:

/objective

Referencing in a handoff: When you run /handoff with an active objective in context, the handoff links back to it automatically. Future sessions can open the objective to understand the broader goal before reading the tactical handoff.

Updating an objective:

/objective update 1 "Migrate auth to JWT; RS256 algorithm confirmed, refresh token scope still open"

Objectives are point-of-truth documents — not status dashboards. They record the goal and its key constraints. Daily status belongs in handoffs.

docs.title: 4.4 Starting a New Session from a Handoff
docs.text:
When you start a session on a topic that has a handoff, pass the handoff filename directly at the start of the conversation:

C:\Users\you\horizon\handoffs\2026-06-22_141500_my-project.md

The agent reads the handoff silently into context — it does not reprint the document, which would bloat context and bury the useful output. Instead, it gives you a 2–4 line orientation: what was being worked on and where to start. You do not need to re-explain context.

If the handoff references an active objective, load it directly:

/objective 3

The objective provides the durable goal; the handoff provides the tactical state. Together they restore full context in seconds.

docs.title: 4.5 The Objective-Handoff Chain
docs.text:
For sustained work spanning more than a couple of sessions, use handoffs and objectives together:

  1. Create an objective at the start of the engagement: /objective create "Migrate the authentication system to JWT by Q3"
  2. Run /handoff at the end of each session. With an active objective in context, the handoff links to it automatically — the handoff's Objective: field carries the number, name, and file path.
  3. Start the next session by reading the handoff. Its Objective: field points to the objective file. Reading the objective restores the durable goal; reading the handoff restores the tactical state.
  4. Update the objective when something significant changes: /objective update 1 "JWT migration: RS256 algorithm confirmed, refresh token scope still open"

The chain persists as long as you run /handoff at the end of each session. The objective link carries forward automatically across as many sessions as the work takes.

docs.title: 4.6 Ephemeral by Design — What Gets Pruned and When
docs.text:
Handoffs and objectives are intentionally ephemeral — treat them like /tmp or perishable goods: useful while fresh, a liability if left to accumulate. They are not designed for backup, archiving, or long-term storage. Older handoffs lose accuracy as the codebase evolves; objectives that go unupdated should retire. If a piece of context matters long-term, it belongs in the brain's memory system or a committed document — not in a handoff.

Both handoffs and objectives are machine-local and gitignored. Neither is committed, synced, or backed up by Horizon.AIOS. They exist only in $HORIZON_ROOT/handoffs/ and $HORIZON_ROOT/objectives/ on this machine. Before uninstalling or migrating Horizon.AIOS, clean up stale handoffs and objectives rather than attempting to back them up as a matter of routine.

Handoffs have no built-in pruning. They accumulate until you clean them manually. Old handoffs become stale naturally — they are point-in-time snapshots, not living documents.

Objectives are pruned by age. horizon_aios_maintain_logs.py removes objectives whose files have not been modified within the AIOS_OBJECTIVES_MAX_DAYS retention window. Key behaviors: - Only /objective update refreshes the modification time. /objective show does not — reading an objective does not reset the clock. - There is no "complete" or "close" command. An objective retires by going unupdated past the retention window. - The objectives/index.md is never pruned regardless of age.

Security note for Blue Team and enterprise operators: Handoffs and objectives are user-generated session summaries that — when produced — give administrators readable visibility into what users summarized at the end of AI sessions. This is user-behavior-dependent: a user who does not run /handoff produces no summary. The ContextThreshold hook (which powers Horizon.AIOS sounds and status line updates) fires at context percentage thresholds but cannot inject into the user's session or force a skill invocation — automated /handoff at threshold is not achievable without user action. Full session-level audit requires CLI-level logging from the harness vendor's tooling. Organizations can establish policy or training norms around /handoff usage to improve coverage, but this is a governance recommendation, not a technical enforcement.

docs.title: 5. Case Study — The Developer Brain
docs.text: This case study illustrates how a developer builds a brain for code review and development assistance, and how the day-to-day workflow operates.

docs.title: 5.1 The Setup
docs.text:
Goal: Separate AI development tooling from the development environment itself. The brain holds all code conventions, review logic, and scripting. The code lives in project folders. The developer uses the brain to assist with their development work, and automated agentic flows use the brain to monitor PRs.

Brain name: code_review

Project folders: - $HORIZON_ROOT/backend-api/ — the primary backend code repo - $HORIZON_ROOT/frontend/ — the frontend repo

docs.title: 5.2 Provisioning the Brain
docs.text:
# Windows, Administrator PowerShell
python "$env:HORIZON_SYSTEM\sbin\horizon_aios_create_brain.py" code_review --automation scheduled

The --automation scheduled flag grants the SeBatchLogonRight so a Task Scheduler task can run the harness as code_review without a user being logged in. This is used for the PR-monitoring agentic flow.

docs.title: 5.3 Configuring the Brain's Workspace
docs.text:
After provisioning, the brain's workspace at $HORIZON_ROOT/brains/code_review/ receives:

1. Code conventions and requirements (brains/code_review/conventions/)

The developer drops all their coding conventions, style guides, architecture decision records, and review checklists as Markdown files here. These become the brain's domain knowledge.

brains/code_review/
  conventions/
    style_guide.md
    api_design_principles.md
    test_requirements.md
    security_checklist.md
    architecture_decisions.md

2. Brain identity (brains/code_review/brain_core.md)

Edit the Role section of the provisioned template:

## Role
You are the code review brain for [developer name]. Your expertise covers:
- This codebase's conventions — see `$BRAIN_HOME/conventions/`
- API design patterns documented in `conventions/api_design_principles.md`
- Security requirements in `conventions/security_checklist.md`

When reviewing code, always apply the full conventions set. When assisting with
development, your working context is the project the user names at session start.

3. Granting project access

# Grant the brain read access to both project folders
icacls "$env:HORIZON_ROOT\backend-api" /grant "code_review:(OI)(CI)RX"
icacls "$env:HORIZON_ROOT\frontend" /grant "code_review:(OI)(CI)RX"

# Create navigation symlinks inside the brain workspace (optional but convenient; run as administrator)
New-Item -ItemType SymbolicLink -Path "$env:HORIZON_ROOT\brains\code_review\backend-api" `
  -Target "$env:HORIZON_ROOT\backend-api"
New-Item -ItemType SymbolicLink -Path "$env:HORIZON_ROOT\brains\code_review\frontend" `
  -Target "$env:HORIZON_ROOT\frontend"

4. Model preferences for the automated PR flow

The agentic PR-monitoring flow does status checks — a task that does not need a powerful model. In brains/code_review/.claude/ (or in the OS-level extend file), configure the PR-check flows to use a token-conscious group:

In the brain's brain_core.md or in the agentic prompt that drives the scheduled task:

Use #lowcost for PR status checks and summaries. Use #highcap when doing
a full review pass with security checklist application.

Or in the model-prefs extend file (see /model-prefs):

## Task-Class Routing
- pr status checks, inbox triage, summaries -> #lowcost
- full code review, security audit          -> #highcap

docs.title: 5.4 The Automated PR-Monitoring Flow
docs.text:
The scheduled task runs daily (or on a trigger) as the code_review OS user. It launches the harness with a non-interactive prompt:

# Registered in Task Scheduler, runs as code_review user
claude -p "Check the status of open PRs in backend-api. For each PR: summarize the change, flag any that have been open more than 3 days without activity, and note any that touch the auth module. Write the summary to $BRAIN_HOME/reports/pr_status_$(date +%Y%m%d).md"

Because the task runs as code_review, it: - Has read access to backend-api/ (explicitly granted) - Has write access to brains/code_review/ (its own workspace) - Cannot touch the Horizon.AIOS system layer (Deny ACE enforced by OS) - Cannot access the owner's memory or other brains (isolation boundary)

The harness runs with the brain's brain_core.md loaded — it knows the codebase conventions without the developer re-explaining them.

docs.title: 5.5 The Interactive Development Workflow
docs.text:
When the developer sits down to work, they log into their own user account (not the brain). They open Claude Code, which runs as themselves. When they want to use the brain for development assistance:

runas /user:code_review claude

Claude opens in the code_review user context, with the brain's full conventions and memory loaded. The developer tells it which codebase they are working on:

"I'm working on the backend-api today. Specifically I'm implementing the new rate-limiting middleware."

The brain navigates to backend-api/ (via the symlink in its workspace), loads the relevant conventions, and assists with development. All agentic work — file reads, writes (within the project folder), searches — happens within the brain's permission boundary. Any files the brain creates or modifies land in the project folder, which is where the developer's git history tracks them.

At the end of the session, the developer runs /handoff to capture where the implementation stands. If there is an active objective ("Implement rate limiting for the v2 API"), the handoff links back to it.

docs.title: 5.6 Why This Works
docs.text:
• Conventions stay with the brain. The developer does not re-paste style guides or architecture docs into every conversation. They live in the brain's workspace, loaded at session start.
  • Code stays in project repos. The brain has access to read the code and write to the project folder, but the project's git history is independent. The developer's normal git workflow is unchanged.
  • Automated flows run as the brain. The scheduled PR check is a real OS-level isolation boundary — not a software promise. The PR-checking process cannot access anything outside its provisioned scope.
  • Token cost is managed. Routine agentic flows use #lowcost. Full review passes escalate to #highcap. The cost is proportionate to the task's reasoning requirement.

docs.title: 5.7 Case Study — The Documentation Brain (Live Example)
docs.text: The brain you are interacting with right now — example_Horizon.AIOS_Docs_Brain — is a working demonstration of how a brain is configured, and what it can and cannot do.

docs.title: What it is
docs.text: This brain is an example brain with no OS user account associated with it. It was not provisioned via horizon_aios_create_brain.py and has no ACL-enforced isolation boundary — it exists purely to demonstrate brain configuration structure and to serve as a practical reference for new users exploring Horizon.AIOS. It runs in the owner's session context rather than as a dedicated brain OS user.

docs.title: What it demonstrates
docs.text:
Brain configuration structure. The brain's identity, role, and behaviors are defined in brain_core.md in its workspace. That file tells it who it is (a documentation assistant for Horizon.AIOS), what its default task is (answer questions about the system), and where to look for information (the documentation index at $HORIZON_DOCS/index.md). This is the pattern every brain follows — the brain_core.md is the brain's standing brief.

Practical documentation search. When you ask this brain a question, its first action is to read the documentation index and then retrieve the relevant documents. It does not answer from training data alone — it reads the actual installed documentation on your system, which means its answers reflect your specific installation. This is the intended usage pattern for a documentation brain: read first, answer from what is there.

Scoped knowledge. This brain is pointed at $HORIZON_DOCS and its own Knowledge/ directory. It does not range outside those boundaries. That is a configuration choice enforced by its brain_core.md instructions, not by OS ACLs — because this brain has no associated OS user account.

docs.title: What would be different with a provisioned brain user
docs.text:
If this brain were running as a dedicated OS user account (as a production brain would be), the filesystem isolation would be enforced by the OS rather than by instruction:

  • It would have no access to $HORIZON_SYSTEM/sbin, skills_sbin, or logs — Deny ACEs applied at provisioning time, non-overridable regardless of what the harness requests
  • It would have no access to other brains' workspaces, the owner's memory, or any project folder not explicitly granted
  • It would have read + execute only on $HORIZON_BIN and skills_bin — it could use shared tooling but not modify it
  • Any attempt by the harness to read or write outside the brain's provisioned boundary would be denied at the OS level, not caught by the agent

The example brain relies on well-written brain_core.md instructions to stay within scope. A production brain relies on the OS. The security posture of a production brain is therefore independent of how well the instructions are followed.

docs.title: 6. Bring Your Own Harness and local.agents.md
6.1 What BYOH Means in Practice
docs.text:
BYOH means Horizon.AIOS does not prescribe which AI system you use. You bring the harness (Claude Code, Codex, Ollama, or anything that can read agents.md), the model (any the harness supports), and the tooling (MCP servers, scripts, APIs).

Horizon.AIOS provides the structure: the standard directory layout, the cross-harness agent instruction conventions (agents.md), the security model, and the configuration management layer. These have no harness dependency.

Harnesses do not need to be manually connected to Horizon.AIOS. They inherit context from the Horizon.AIOS directory structure automatically — because Horizon.AIOS onboarding redirects the harness's default configuration directory (e.g., ~/.claude/) to point at the Horizon.AIOS root. After that, every harness launch in the Horizon.AIOS environment picks up the full context chain without additional wiring. "Bring your own harness" means the harness is a guest in the Horizon.AIOS layer, not that you must integrate it.

The current implementation is fully wired for Claude Code. Other harnesses (Codex, Ollama, LM Studio) have stubs in $HORIZON_SYSTEM/harness_configs/ but are not yet fully integrated. See $HORIZON_DOCS/philosophy.md §9 for the honest current-state assessment.

docs.title: 6.2 The Configuration Chain
docs.text:
Agent instructions load in a specific order, from most global to most local. Understanding this chain is how you configure a brain or a project without touching the shared OS layer:

~/.claude/CLAUDE.md              ← thin stub; points at the OS repo
  ↓ @-imports
$HORIZON_ROOT/.claude/CLAUDE.md  ← project harness entry; imports root CLAUDE.md
  ↓ @-imports
$HORIZON_ROOT/CLAUDE.md          ← Horizon.AIOS-wide rules; imports agents.md
  ↓ @-imports
$HORIZON_ROOT/agents.md          ← cross-harness agent instructions
  ↓ @-imports
horizon_aios_agents.md           ← OS-layer agent config (skills, orchestration, session-start)
  ↓ @-imports
local.agents.md                  ← machine-local overrides (gitignored)

For a brain, the chain adds one more layer at the innermost level:

brains/<name>/.claude/CLAUDE.md  ← thin entry; @-imports brain_core.md
brains/<name>/brain_core.md      ← brain identity, persona, scope restrictions

docs.title: 6.3 local.agents.md — The Primary Configuration Seam
docs.text:
local.agents.md is the gitignored override file at the end of the agents.md chain. It is the right place for any instruction that: - Is specific to this machine or this owner's preferences - Should not ship to other users or machines - Overrides or extends shipped behavior

aios setup creates it from the template at $HORIZON_ROOT/local.agents.md and $HORIZON_ROOT/.claude/local.agents.md. Edit it directly. It is never overwritten by syncs or updates.

Examples of what belongs in local.agents.md:

# Local Agent Instructions

## Git
- When the user says "commit", always commit AND push. Do not stop at the local commit.

## Session Behavior
- This machine is the code review workstation. Default the working project to backend-api
  unless the user specifies otherwise.

## Developer Identity
- Owner: Jane Smith. GitHub: @jsmith. GPG key fingerprint: ABCD1234...

Keep it short. local.agents.md loads into context every session. Every line costs tokens.

docs.title: 6.4 Adding Support for a New Harness
docs.text:
To add a new harness to Horizon.AIOS:

1.1 Create $HORIZON_SYSTEM/harness_configs/<harness-name>/ for runtime config (hooks, sounds map, README). 1.2 Create $HORIZON_SYSTEM/templates/<harness-name>/ for setup templates copied at bootstrap. 1.3 Add an equivalent to the Claude Code settings.json template for the new harness's config format. 1.4 Wire hooks to the sounds system in $HORIZON_SYSTEM/sounds/. 1.5 Document the harness in $HORIZON_DOCS/.

The brain model, ACL layer, and OS-level isolation are reusable as-is. The work is in the integration scaffolding, not the OS layer.

See $HORIZON_ETC/ai_os_personalizations.md §3 for the full harness addition protocol and $HORIZON_DOCS/getting_started/ReadMeToSetupYourSystem.md → "Adding a New AI Harness."

docs.title: 7.1 Model Preferences — Routing Work to the Right Model
docs.text:
The model-preference layer lets you control which model handles different kinds of work — without changing your interactive session model. There is no resolver script, no engine, no env-var wiring. The mechanism is in-context instruction: you write a configuration file, Horizon.AIOS loads it each session, and the acting model honors it when spawning agents or delegating tasks.

Model groups are the core abstraction: named lists of models that define the capability tier for a class of work. The shipped groups:

Group Intended use #lowcost Routine, mechanical work — status checks, summaries, formatting #midcost Standard development and writing tasks #highcap Complex reasoning, cross-document synthesis, architecture decisions #investigate Research-heavy tasks requiring deep comprehension #debug Debugging passes, root cause analysis #fast Latency-sensitive operations where speed matters more than depth

The system selects the first runnable member of the group in your current runtime. Members that do not match your runtime (e.g., an Ollama model in a Claude Code session) are silently skipped — so you can define groups that include both Anthropic and local models and the right one is used automatically based on what is available.

What the preference layer governs. Your interactive session model is set by the harness at launch and is not changed by this configuration. What it governs — for the first time — is the model used for spawned agents and delegated tasks. Routine delegated work runs on a cheap model; complex delegated work escalates automatically. Cost becomes proportionate to task reasoning requirements, decided by your configuration rather than left to defaults.

Task-class routing directs entire categories of work to the right tier automatically:

## Task-Class Routing
- pr status checks, inbox triage, summaries -> #lowcost
- full code review, security audit          -> #highcap

Setting up model preferences:

Run /model-prefs to configure interactively, or set up the extend file manually:

# Linux / macOS
cp "$HORIZON_ETC/horizon_aios_model_prefs.local.template.md" \
   "$HORIZON_ETC/horizon_aios_model_prefs.local.md"

# Windows
Copy-Item "$env:HORIZON_ETC\horizon_aios_model_prefs.local.template.md" `
          "$env:HORIZON_ETC\horizon_aios_model_prefs.local.md"

All choices go in this gitignored file — never in the OS-tracked base spec, which a sync would overwrite. Run /model-catalog-refresh to fetch current model IDs and pricing before filling in group members.

Configuration cascades by scope: OS-global < project root < brain workspace, most-specific wins. A brain or project can override the global config by dropping its own extend file in its directory.

See $HORIZON_DOCS/system/model_preferences.md for the full member grammar, scope cascade, and configuration walkthrough.

docs.title: 7.2 Agent Teams — Structured Multi-Agent Workflows
docs.text:
Agent teams let you define coordinated multi-agent workflows with specific roles, model assignments, and loop constructs — invoked by name rather than assembled from scratch each time.

A team definition specifies: - Roles — the participants, their charters, and their model groups - Loop constructs — loop-back conditions, pass/fail criteria, and iteration caps that govern when agents hand off to each other

Four starter teams ship with Horizon.AIOS and are ready to use immediately:

Team Purpose Investigate & Fix Diagnose a problem then apply the fix Full Team Full lifecycle for a sizable or ambiguous task (default for "send an agent team") Review & Fix Audit a diff then apply findings Explore & Summarize Fan out across a codebase or question then distill

Using a team:

Name the team at the start of a task that fits its pattern. Natural language is the interface; the acting model maps your request to the team definition and its SAILL flags:

  1. "Send an investigate-and-fix team at the flaky test in auth/." → Investigator (#midcost) diagnoses → Fixer (#lowcost) patches.

  2. "Send a full-team to build the export feature, but ask me before you start implementing." → Planner gates on ask user — presents the approach and waits for approval before the Implementer runs.

  3. "Run a review-and-fix over my current diff, but only fix if I say so." → Reviewer (#highcap) audits → Fixer (#lowcost, if asked) applies findings only on your explicit approval.

  4. "Explore-and-summarize how sessions persist — fan out three explorers in parallel across the API, the DB, and the UI, then summarize." → Three Explorers (#investigate, parallel) → Summarizer (#lowcost, wait) syncs and distills.

  5. "Send a full-team, but skip the log-reader and loop the validator up to 3 times until clean." → Log-reader (if needed) is skipped → Validator loops back to the Implementer until it passes or hits 3 iterations.

Directing roles in plain language:

A team is a scaffold, not a cage. At invocation you can also direct a specific role — what to scope to, which skill to run, what to focus on — and the model honors it on top of the team definition:

"Send a full-team for the export bug; have the Validator run /security-review, and Log-reader, scope to just the auth module."

The distinction: SAILL flags govern control flow — whether, when, how often, and in what order a role runs. Role direction is charter prose — what work the role actually does. Direction is plain language at invocation; SAILL is the compact notation in the team definition. The two compose cleanly; do not encode role direction as SAILL flags.

Defining your own team:

Create or edit local.agent_teams.md in any scope. The innermost scope wins. Example:

### security-review-team
Independent security audit of changed code.

1. Auditor A (`#investigate`, parallel) — injection risks and input validation
2. Auditor B (`#investigate`, parallel) — credential and secrets handling
3. Synthesizer (`#highcap`, wait) — consolidated findings with remediation priority

Run /agent-teams to manage team definitions interactively. See $HORIZON_DOCS/system/agent_teams.md for the full invocation pattern, scope cascade, and loop/retry grammar.

docs.title: 7.3 Loop / Retry Constructs
docs.text:
A role in an agent team can declare a Loop to re-run an earlier role with feedback until a pass condition is met or an iteration cap is reached. Declare it inline below the role:

Loop: on <condition>, return feedback to "<role name>" and re-run from there; repeat until <pass condition> or <max> iterations, then <action at cap>.

Four things a looping role must specify:

  1. Condition — what counts as a failure and triggers another pass (e.g., "validation fails").
  2. Loop-back target — the earlier role to re-run, by name (preferred over step number so renumbering never breaks the target).
  3. Max iterations — a hard cap that bounds cost and guarantees termination.
  4. Cap action — what to do if the cap is hit without passing: report-failure (stop and surface outstanding failures) or proceed (continue with a caveat). Prefer report-failure.

The full-team Validator is the canonical example: if it fails, it returns specific feedback to the Implementer and re-runs from there — up to 3 iterations, then stops and reports rather than proceeding with broken output.

docs.title: 7.4 Conditional Roles
docs.text:
A role may be marked conditional by appending a flag inside its model-group parenthetical:

  • (#group, if needed) — the acting model runs the role only when it judges the role adds value for the task; otherwise it skips to the next.
  • (#group, if asked) — the role runs only when the user explicitly requests it (e.g., "…and validate it"); skipped by default.

Conditions combine with loops: a conditional looping role loops only on runs where it actually executes.

The full-team Log-reader is an example: (#lowcost, if needed) — skipped for tasks that need no runtime evidence.

docs.title: 7.5 SAILL — Standardized AI Loop Language
docs.text:
The flags and loop constructs above are part of SAILL (Standardized AI Loop Language) — a small, vendor-neutral vocabulary for expressing how a team runs, not just who is in it. A SAILL-annotated team definition is portable: the same notation is readable by a human, the acting model, and the resolve_agent_teams.py tooling.

The flag vocabulary is cataloged in $HORIZON_ETC/agent_team_flags.md, loaded every session. Current flags:

Flag Form Meaning if needed inline Run only if it adds value; else skip if asked inline Run only when the user explicitly requests it; else skip ask user inline Pause and wait for the user's input, decision, or approval before continuing parallel inline Run concurrently with adjacent parallel roles wait inline Wait for the preceding parallel group to finish (sync point) Loop annotation Re-run an earlier role with feedback until pass/cap (see §7.3)

SAILL flags are primitives — small, atomic, and composable. Like the closed-class control words of a programming language (if, while, parallel, await), the set is intentionally small; expressive power comes from composing them in a team definition, not adding bespoke flags. Each primitive does exactly one thing, combines cleanly with the others, and has a single registry-defined meaning.

Sub-teams and nesting — boxes:

A box [ … ] bundles one or more roles into a single node that runs as a unit. Name a box to declare an inline sub-team — Recon[ … ]. Boxes nest without limit; the bracket structure sets the order of operations. All the ordinary SAILL flags apply inside a box — no new syntax:

# Parallel recon, then plan:
Orchestrator (#highcap) ->
  Recon[ APICrawler (#investigate, parallel), DBReader (#investigate, parallel) ] (wait) ->
  Planner (#highcap)

# Gated build with an implement/validate loop:
Planner (#highcap, ask user) ->
  [ Implementer (#lowcost), Validator (#midcost) Loop: to "Implementer" until pass or 3 ]

# Parallel sub-teams, then integrate (nesting):
[ Frontend[ Impl (#lowcost), Val (#midcost) Loop: to "Impl" until pass or 3 ] (parallel),
  Backend[  Impl (#lowcost), Val (#midcost) Loop: to "Impl" until pass or 3 ] (parallel) ]
(wait) -> Integrator (#midcost)

Context values (-context-):

Wherever a SAILL parameter would take a literal — a loop's pass condition, a cap, a scope, a target — write -context- to mean "resolve this from the user's invocation or runtime state." Qualify it with a name: -context:pass criteria-, -context:cap-, -context:source data-. This keeps team definitions generic and reusable — the structure is fixed; the specifics arrive at invocation time:

# Loop until a context-defined pass condition, capped at a literal 3:
[ Implementer (#lowcost), Validator (#midcost)
  Loop: to "Implementer" until -context:pass criteria- or 3 ]

# Scope a role from context (in the role's charter):
Investigator (#midcost) — audit -context:source data-

Compound exits, failure handlers, and skill calls:

A Loop may list several or-separated exit conditions: a pass condition, ask user (the user approves or stops early), and a cap. A role may declare an if fail <action> handler that runs when the role fails or a loop hits its cap unmet — the action is typically a skill call. Skill calls (slash-named) belong in a role's charter as work direction, not as SAILL control-flow flags:

# Compound loop exit — clean, or user decides, or cap:
Security-Reviewer (#highcap) — audit -context:source data-;
  on findings Loop: to "Build" until clean or ask user or -context:cap-

# Failure handler with a skill call:
Build[ Implementer (#lowcost), Validator (#midcost) Loop: to "Implementer" until pass or 3 ]
  if fail /build_fail_triage_report

Extending the flag vocabulary: add flags in local.agent_team_flags.md (gitignored, any scope) or via /agent-teams. The resolver parses flags generically — a new term works as soon as it appears in the file:

resolve_agent_teams.py --flags

Do not edit $HORIZON_ETC/agent_team_flags.md directly; it is synced from upstream. Use local.agent_team_flags.md for local extensions and propose community-useful terms upstream. SAILL's goal is a shared, open language for agentic workflows across harnesses and projects — not a Horizon-only dialect. Adopt it, extend it, and contribute improvements back.

See $HORIZON_DOCS/system/agent_teams.md for the full SAILL specification including the complete primitives reference and naming conventions.

docs.title: 7.6 Agent Team Skills
docs.text:
Three owner skills support the agent-team and SAILL workflow end-to-end:

Skill What it does /agent-teams Lists teams in effect for the current path; creates or edits team definitions at any scope (OS, project, brain, subfolder); adds custom SAILL flags /test-agent-teams End-to-end self-test: spawns each role of every defined team, each echoes a nonce + role name + charter + actual model; reports per-team PASS/FAIL for resolution and model routing /convert-prompt-to-saill Converts a natural-language prompt into a SAILL agent-team flow (roles, model groups, flags, sub-team boxes, loops, -context- values); outputs the SAILL block with a gloss, then offers to save or run it

Use /test-agent-teams after defining or modifying a team to confirm that roles resolve to the expected models before you invoke the team on real work. Use /convert-prompt-to-saill ad hoc to turn any workflow you can describe in plain language into SAILL, then feed that output into /agent-teams to populate a custom agent team — the on-ramp from "a workflow in my head" to standardized SAILL to a reusable named team (verify it with /test-agent-teams).

See $HORIZON_DOCS/system/agent_teams.md for the full SAILL spec, scope cascade, and team-definition grammar.

docs.title: 8.1 What Context Is and Why It Costs
docs.text:
Every Claude Code session pays a fixed token cost before you type anything. The harness assembles a system prompt from files on disk. Every byte in those files is billed on every session — whether Claude uses it or not.

The practical implication: the larger your auto-loaded context, the more every session costs. The Horizon.AIOS baseline (excluding user-global ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md) is approximately 3,660 tokens at the Horizon.AIOS root. This is the fixed cost of running the OS layer. Everything you add on top of this is your cost to manage. Run python $HORIZON_BIN/context_cost.py $HORIZON_ROOT for the current number — it drifts as files are edited.

docs.title: 8.2 What Gets Loaded in Any Given Directory
docs.text:
Claude Code loads CLAUDE.md files from ~/.claude/ down to the current working directory — all of them, stacked. Then it resolves all @-imports recursively.

The files loaded in a standard Horizon.AIOS session starting at $HORIZON_ROOT:

File Purpose ~Tokens ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md User-global; points at the OS repo varies $HORIZON_ROOT/.claude/CLAUDE.md Thin entry; imports root CLAUDE.md 7 $HORIZON_ROOT/CLAUDE.md Horizon.AIOS-wide rules; imports agents.md ~7 $HORIZON_ROOT/agents.md Cross-harness instructions ~291 horizon_aios_agents.md OS-layer agent config ~777 horizon_aios_model_prefs.md Model preference spec ~1367 agent_teams.md Agent team definitions ~1012 agent_team_flags.md Role-flag vocabulary ~198 local.agents.md Machine-local overrides varies

Run /context-cost or python $HORIZON_BIN/context_cost.py $HORIZON_ROOT for current numbers — counts drift as files are edited. The Horizon.AIOS baseline (excluding user-global) is approximately 3,660 tokens.

If you start a session inside $HORIZON_ROOT/my-project/, any CLAUDE.md in my-project/ or my-project/.claude/ is added on top. If you are running as a brain, the brain's CLAUDE.md is the innermost layer.

docs.title: 8.3 Measuring Context Overhead
docs.text:
To see exactly what is loaded in any directory and how much it costs:

python "$HORIZON_SYSTEM/bin/context_cost.py" [path]

Or run /context-cost inside a Claude Code session (available to brains and owner alike).

Output: per-file KB, word count, and estimated token count, plus a total. Thresholds: - ≥ 1,000 tokens: note (moderate — review before adding more) - ≥ 2,000 tokens: warning (high — trim before adding more)

Run context_cost.py after adding or modifying any CLAUDE.md or @-import. Confirm the overhead stayed in budget before committing.

docs.title: 8.4 The @-Import Rule — Unconditional Loading
docs.text:
A critical misconception: @file in CLAUDE.md does not lazy-load. The harness inlines the file's full content into the system prompt before the session starts, unconditionally. Writing "only load if needed" on the same line as an @-import does not help — those words become part of the already-loaded content.

For content you want available only when needed: do not @-import it. Instead, tell Claude in prose where to find it:

If you need the API design principles, read `$BRAIN_HOME/conventions/api_design_principles.md`.

That costs a few tokens in the always-loaded context. The file itself costs nothing until Claude reads it mid-conversation.

Only CLAUDE.md and CLAUDE.local.md trigger @-import resolution. agents.md @-references are passed to Claude as plain text — the harness does not inline them.

See $HORIZON_DOCS/authoring/claude_md_authoring.md for the full authoring reference and $HORIZON_DOCS/context_loading.md for the complete loading mechanics.

docs.title: 8.5 Per-Layer Configuration Guide
docs.text: Layer Edit when Keep ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md User-global preferences that apply across all projects Minimal — it loads in every session including unrelated projects $HORIZON_ROOT/.claude/CLAUDE.md Never — thin entry point only One line: @$HORIZON_ROOT/CLAUDE.md $HORIZON_ROOT/agents.md Horizon.AIOS-wide cross-harness instructions Rules that apply to every session in every project local.agents.md Machine-local overrides Short — loads every session brains/<name>/.claude/CLAUDE.md Brain persona, scope, memory pointers Short — loads every brain session my-project/.claude/CLAUDE.md Project-specific context Only what Claude cannot derive from the code

docs.title: 8.6 Health Check
docs.text:
To verify the full Horizon.AIOS installation and configuration is correct:

# From a Claude Code session
/doctor

# From the command line
python "$HORIZON_SYSTEM/sbin/horizon_aios_doctor.py"

This checks env vars, the skills symlink, git hooks, the Horizon.AIOS registry, and the Deny ACLs on sbin, skills_sbin, and logs.

docs.title: 9.1 What It Is
docs.text:
The Terseness Contract is a set of authoring rules that governs every file loaded unconditionally into every Horizon.AIOS session — the always-loaded chain. Because each byte in those files is billed on every session for every user and every brain, verbosity in them is a cost imposed on all interactions Horizon.AIOS will ever have.

The always-loaded files include agents.md, horizon_aios_agents.md, horizon_aios_model_prefs.md, agent_teams.md, agent_team_flags.md, and the @-import chain that wires them together. The authoritative index of which files are in-scope is $HORIZON_DOCS/terseness_contract_index.md.

docs.title: 9.2 The Seven Criteria
docs.text:
A file in the always-loaded chain passes the Terseness Contract if, and only if:

  1. Every line earns its keep. Removing it would break the file's function or leave a required instruction unspecified.
  2. Instructions are imperative, not discursive. Tell the model what to do. Rationale belongs in dev_values.md or philosophy.md.
  3. No rationale that belongs elsewhere. If the "why" is in a higher-authority doc, cut the prose and use a pointer.
  4. No inline examples when a pointer suffices. "See $HORIZON_DOCS/X.md" is cheaper than an inline example.
  5. No redundancy with sibling always-loaded files. If another indexed file already says it, one copy is the defect.
  6. No commented-out content. Dead lines and # TODO prose still cost tokens.
  7. @-imports only for always-needed files. @-importing a large reference doc "for convenience" is a fail. Use a prose pointer instead.

docs.title: 9.3 Checking Compliance
docs.text:
Run the terseness check from any owner session:

/terseness-check

The skill evaluates each tracked file in the index against the seven criteria and reports PASS or FAIL per criterion per file. Files in the gitignored / user-controlled section of the index are checked and reported as ADVISORY — the user bears the cost of their own verbosity, so fixes are not applied without prompting.

Run /terseness-check after adding or modifying any always-loaded file or @-import. Confirm all tracked files pass before committing.

docs.title: 9.4 The Terseness Contract Index
docs.text:
The authoritative file list with loading paths and per-file constraints is:

$HORIZON_DOCS/terseness_contract_index.md

Update it whenever a file is added to or removed from the always-loaded chain (a new @-import in any indexed file, or a new CLAUDE.md layer inserted into the harness loading path). The index is the contract's scope boundary — if a file is not listed, it is not subject to the strict contract (though always-loaded files added without updating the index will be caught on the next /terseness-check pass as a gap finding).

docs.title: 10. Gitignore, Local Config, and What Not to Commit
10.1 The Two-Gitignore Model
docs.text:
Horizon.AIOS uses two gitignore mechanisms that serve different purposes:

.gitignore — tracked, committed, ships with every clone. Contains patterns for OS-layer artifacts that should never be tracked on any machine (build outputs, temp files, the generated active_env files, memory/, handoffs/).

.gitignore.user — machine-local, gitignored itself, never committed. Contains patterns specific to this machine and this user's personal exclusions. Only .gitignore.user.template (the empty starting template) is tracked.

The pre-commit hook syncs .gitignore.user to .git/info/exclude on every commit. To add a personal exclusion pattern:

# Edit $HORIZON_ROOT/.gitignore.user
MyPersonalNotes/
scratch/
RedTeam/

The pattern takes effect on the next commit — no manual sync step. The file is machine-local and does not travel with a clone.

docs.title: 10.2 What Is Never Committed
docs.text:
These files are gitignored and must stay that way:

File / Directory What it holds local.agents.md (all instances) Machine-local agent instructions; overrides shipped content settings.local.json Machine-specific permission overrides horizon_aios_model_prefs.local.md Your private model groups and routing rules memory/ Harness transcripts and agent memory (never publish) handoffs/ Session handoff documents objectives/ Durable objectives usrbin/usr_skills/ Machine-local personal skills brains/<name>/ Brain workspaces (sensitive — personal credentials, memory) $HORIZON_SYSTEM/logs/ Audit and event logs aios_local.conf Local sync and log configuration ~/.claude/settings.json Global settings (points at machine-specific wrapper paths) ai_os_etc/git_identity.local.gitconfig Machine-local git identity

docs.title: 10.3 Protecting Configuration Points — The Clobber Hazard
docs.text:
An upstream sync (git pull / aios sync) will update the OS layer. The files it can overwrite are tracked files: agents.md, horizon_system/, the shipped templates.

What it cannot overwrite: anything gitignored. This is why your customizations belong in the gitignored seam files:

  • Override agents.md behavior → use local.agents.md
  • Override model preferences → use the gitignored extend.md
  • Override permissions → use settings.local.json
  • Override session behavior per-machine → use local.agents.md

Never put machine-specific or personal configuration into the tracked files. A sync will clobber it, and other users' clones will break.

If you want to ship a configuration change to all users (or to your own other machines), it belongs in the tracked files. If you want it only on this machine, it belongs in the gitignored files.

docs.title: 10.4 Checking What a Sync Would Overwrite
docs.text:
Before pulling:

git fetch origin
git diff HEAD..origin/master -- agents.md .claude/CLAUDE.md horizon_system/

Review the diff. If the upstream changed a file you have also customized, the update applies cleanly because your customizations are in the gitignored seam files — not in the tracked files. This is the purpose of the local.agents.md / extend.md pattern.

See $HORIZON_DOCS/system/distribution_and_updates.md for the full framework vs. user-space model and $HORIZON_DOCS/getting_started/updating.md for the step-by-step update procedure.

docs.title: 11. Enterprise Deployment
docs.text: Day-one sequence for an enterprise deployment: 1. Fork HorizonBrute/Horizon_AI_OS into your organization's Git infrastructure. 2. In the org fork, commit your policy into agents.md, horizon_system/ai_os_etc/security_invariants.md, and horizon_system/templates/ (brain template, settings.json template). 3. Set the org fork as the clone source for all employee machines. 4. Run the bootstrap on each machine (via Intune/SCCM, Ansible, or manually): git clone <org-fork-url> && powershell -File horizon_system/sbin/bootstrap.ps1 (Windows) or bash horizon_system/sbin/bootstrap.sh --yes (Linux). 5. Provision brain accounts for any automated workflows: horizon_aios_create_brain.py <name> --automation scheduled. 6. Verify with /doctor from any onboarded session.

docs.title: 11.1 The Model: Organization Fork, Employee Clone
docs.text:
The Horizon.AIOS distribution model is designed for downstream customization without merge conflicts. An enterprise maps directly onto this model:

Official upstream (HorizonBrute/Horizon_AI_OS)
  ↓ fork
Organization repo (your-org/Horizon_AI_OS)    ← org-controlled; org policy lives here
  ↓ clone
Employee machine                               ← employee customizations in gitignored seam files

The organization forks the official upstream repo into their own Git infrastructure (GitHub Enterprise, GitLab, Bitbucket, Azure DevOps). That fork becomes the authoritative source of truth for the organization. Employees clone from the org fork, not from the official upstream. The org controls what ships to every employee; the official upstream controls what the org chooses to pull in.

docs.title: 11.2 Where Organization Policy Lives
docs.text:
The framework/user-space split that protects individual user customizations from being clobbered by syncs works identically at the org level:

File / Layer Who owns it How the org enforces it agents.md Org (in the fork) Org policy committed here ships to every clone horizon_system/ Org (in the fork) Security templates, hook scripts, ACL defaults — all controlled horizon_system/ai_os_etc/security_invariants.md Org (in the fork) The org's authoritative security posture horizon_system/templates/ Org (in the fork) The brain template, settings.json template, profile templates local.agents.md Employee (gitignored) Machine-local only; org cannot ship this settings.local.json Employee (gitignored) Machine-local only; org cannot ship this brains/, memory/ Employee (gitignored) Never reaches the org repo

The org commits policy directly into the tracked framework files in their fork. Employees who sync get the updated policy automatically. Employees' gitignored customizations are never touched.

What belongs in the org's fork: - Organization-wide agent instructions in agents.md (approved tools, prohibited actions, required session behaviors) - Standardized brain templates in horizon_system/templates/ that reflect the org's compliance requirements - Security invariant documents that encode the org's control posture - Approved skill sets in skills_bin/ and skills_sbin/ - Model-preference routing that reflects the org's approved models and cost policy - Hook scripts that write to the org's SIEM or compliance logging infrastructure

docs.title: 11.3 Pulling Upstream Updates into the Org Fork
docs.text:
The org maintains the fork like any upstream-tracking fork:

# In the org fork repo (run by whoever manages the Horizon.AIOS distribution)
git remote add upstream git@github.com:HorizonBrute/Horizon_AI_OS.git
git fetch upstream
git merge upstream/master   # or rebase; review before merging

# After review and any org-specific adjustments:
git push origin master      # employees sync from here

The org can choose how aggressively to track upstream — immediate, quarterly, or only for specific security patches. The org's changes to tracked files (org policy additions to agents.md, etc.) merge with upstream changes normally. As long as the org has not duplicated content that lives in the user-space seam files, merges are clean by construction.

docs.title: 11.4 Centralized Deployment and Force Install
docs.text:
For managed machines, the org can distribute and install Horizon.AIOS through standard enterprise software deployment mechanisms:

Windows (Intune / SCCM / Group Policy): 1.1 Package the clone + bootstrap as a deployment script. Intune Win32 app wraps git clone <org-fork-url> C:\devroot && powershell -File C:\devroot\horizon_system\sbin\bootstrap.ps1. 1.2 The bootstrap runs as the logged-in user for the Claude Code wiring steps, and as SYSTEM for the ACL hardening steps (the bootstrap detects this and handles the split automatically). 1.3 Use a managed GPO to set HORIZON_ROOT as a machine-level environment variable, removing the need for users to configure it. 1.4 Deploy the org's approved settings.json template through the bootstrap — it is already templated for this.

Linux (Ansible / Chef / Puppet / Salt):

# Ansible playbook excerpt
- name: Clone Horizon.AIOS from org fork
  git:
    repo: "git@git.your-org.com:aios/Horizon_AI_OS.git"
    dest: /opt/aios
    version: main

- name: Run Horizon.AIOS bootstrap
  command: bash /opt/aios/horizon_system/sbin/bootstrap.sh --yes
  become: true

After the play runs, the system has a fully configured Horizon.AIOS installation with org-approved configuration. User-specific customization happens afterward through the gitignored seam files.

Note on containerized enterprise deployment: Docker and Kubernetes templates exist in $HORIZON_SYSTEM/templates/docker/ and are documented in §12. The isolation model ports cleanly to containers in theory — the pieces fit together like Lego bricks. However, these templates have not been end-to-end verified across all target environments. Docker on Linux host has partial verification; Windows and macOS hosts, per-brain container isolation, and Kubernetes are untested. Treat the containerization docs as a well-structured starting point, not a production-ready deployment. See $HORIZON_DOCS/deployment/docker.md → Known Gaps before building on them.

docs.title: 11.5 Multi-Operator and Team Environments
docs.text:
On a shared server or in a team environment where multiple humans operate the same Horizon.AIOS installation:

1.1 Each human operator gets their own OS user account. Do not share owner accounts. The global ~/.claude/settings.json (hooks, statusLine) is per OS home directory — it must stay per-operator. 1.2 Each operator runs bootstrap under their own account. Bootstrap installs each operator's personal ~/.claude/ from the org's template. The devroot .claude/settings.json (permissions only) is shared across all operators — that is intentional. 1.3 Brains are AI agent accounts, not human accounts. A team member who needs to interact with a brain does so by running the harness as that brain user, not by sharing the brain's OS account. 1.4 The $HORIZON_ROOT repo can be shared storage or separate clones. Shared storage keeps all operators on the same Horizon.AIOS version automatically; separate clones give each operator independent upgrade control. Both models work. 1.5 Human operator accounts must be explicitly granted access to brain workspaces and project folders they need. In a single-user install this is automatic — the owner has full access to brains/ and $HORIZON_PROJECTS/ because they own the machine. In a multi-user or enterprise deployment, additional operators are real OS accounts that do not own $HORIZON_ROOT. Access to a specific brain workspace (brains/<name>/) or project folder is not inherited. Grant it via icacls (Windows) or setfacl/group membership (Linux), or by adding operators to groups your identity infrastructure already manages (AD security groups, LDAP groups, etc.). Horizon.AIOS tooling provisions brain account ACLs out of the box; it does not provision human operator ACLs — that is outside its portable, IaC-forward scope and must be handled by the administrative context using whatever the organization already operates.

See $HORIZON_DOCS/deployment/server.md → "Multi-Operator Server Pattern" for the full treatment.

docs.title: 11.6 Sync Schedule and Drift Prevention
docs.text:
To keep employee machines current with the org fork without requiring manual pulls:

# Employees add the org fork as their origin (done at clone time):
git clone git@git.your-org.com:aios/Horizon_AI_OS.git C:\devroot

# horizon_aios_sync.py (fast-forward-only) can be scheduled:
python "$HORIZON_SYSTEM/sbin/horizon_aios_sync.py"

The sync script is fast-forward-only by design. If an employee has edited a tracked framework file (violating the user-space principle), the sync refuses rather than silently overwriting. That refusal is the signal to move the change into the correct gitignored seam file. The org can schedule this via Windows Task Scheduler or Linux cron as a background maintenance task.

See $HORIZON_DOCS/system/distribution_and_updates.md for the complete framework vs. user-space model.

docs.title: 12. Containerization, Cloud, and Infrastructure as Code
docs.text:
BEFORE YOU BUILD ON ANY OF THIS: READ THE STATUS NOTE BELOW.

Status: Lego bricks that fit in theory — end-to-end verification not complete.

The Docker templates, Kubernetes manifests, Terraform/cloud-init examples, and per-brain container patterns in this section describe how the pieces should snap together. The isolation model ports cleanly to containers in principle. In practice, only Docker on a Linux host has been partially tested. Windows/macOS hosts (Docker Desktop / WSL2), per-brain container isolation, and Kubernetes are architecturally sound but untested end-to-end. There is little operational experience in this knowledge base with containerized Horizon.AIOS deployments.

Treat everything below as a well-structured starting point — not a production-ready runbook. Read $HORIZON_DOCS/deployment/docker.md → Known Gaps before committing to any of these patterns.

docs.title: 12.1 Why Horizon.AIOS Is IaC-Friendly
docs.text:
Horizon.AIOS is, at its core, a set of files and filesystem ACL rules. There are no daemons that must be running, no databases to migrate, no external state to synchronize. The entire system state is:

1.1 The repository contents (cloned from git — reproducible) 1.2 The bootstrap outputs (symlinks, wrappers, active_env.* — generated by bootstrap.{ps1,sh}) 1.3 The ACL assignments on the filesystem (applied by horizon_aios_harden.py and horizon_aios_create_brain.py) 1.4 The OS user accounts for brains (provisioned by horizon_aios_create_brain.py) 1.5 The credentials in the OS keystore (managed by horizon_aios_brain_credential.py)

This maps directly to standard IaC patterns: declare the desired state, apply it, verify it.

docs.title: 12.2 Docker
docs.text:
Horizon.AIOS ships Docker templates in $HORIZON_SYSTEM/templates/docker/: - Dockerfile — Ubuntu-based image; runs bootstrap as root before switching to the aios user; brains are OS users within the container - docker-compose.yml — single Horizon.AIOS service with named volumes for logs, handoffs, objectives, and an optional host-path bind for brains/ - .dockerignore — excludes memory/, handoffs/, brains/, and logs/ from the build context

# Build and run
docker build -f horizon_system/templates/docker/Dockerfile -t horizon-aios .
docker compose -f horizon_system/templates/docker/docker-compose.yml up -d

# Open a Claude Code session inside the container
docker exec -it horizon-aios claude

Mutable state (logs, handoffs, objectives, brain workspaces) is volume-mounted. The Horizon.AIOS layer itself is baked into the image. After a Horizon.AIOS update, rebuild the image; volumes persist unchanged.
